title: SQL Lab 3: Top 3 Salaries per Department
short_description: Return the top 3 salary bands in each department and keep ties.
business_context: Compensation reviews often need top salary bands per department, and tied salaries must be treated fairly.
problem_statement: Return employees whose salary is in the top 3 distinct salary bands within their department. Keep ties.
student_task: Write a query returning emp_id, dept_id, and salary.
learning_goals: Choose DENSE_RANK when ties should stay
Explain tie behavior before writing SQL
production_logs: interview-sim: dept 10 has two employees tied at rank 2
review-note: ROW_NUMBER forces exactly 3 rows and can drop valid ties
solution_query:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT emp_id,
         dept_id,
         salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk
  FROM employees
)
SELECT emp_id, dept_id, salary
FROM ranked
WHERE rnk <= 3;
explanation: DENSE_RANK assigns the same rank to equal salary values inside each department, so all employees in the top 3 salary bands are returned.
common_mistakes: Using ROW_NUMBER when ties should be kept
Returning rank column when the expected output does not include it
hints: Tie behavior decides the ranking function.
Use DENSE_RANK with PARTITION BY dept_id.
